content.text: Mutation Coverage Quality Gates in CI

A mutation coverage quality gate is a CI check that blocks a merge or release when the mutation score of the eval harness (or the robustness score of the system prompt) falls below a configured threshold. It complements line-coverage gates, which only measure whether a line of code was executed, not whether the assertion attached to it actually catches a fault.

This document covers how to compose mutation gates with other CI gates, how to configure tiered thresholds, how to implement a ratchet rule that prevents score regression, and how to annotate pull requests with actionable mutation data.

attrs.alt: Mutation coverage quality gate pipeline in CI

content.text: Gate Architecture

Mutation suites are slow — each mutant requires a full test run or an LLM call. The practical approach is a multi-tier gate pipeline that fails fast on cheap checks before running the expensive mutation pass:
Fast gate (2–5 min):
lint, type check, unit tests, structural TipTap validation. Every PR.
Mutation gate (20–40 min):
AST-level mutmut run on eval harness code + domain-specific mutation operators. Every PR that touches
eval/
or
prompts/
Robustness gate (10–20 min):
prompt mutation runner scoring wording/structural/injection variants. Triggered by changes to
prompts/
Nightly full suite:
broader mutation sweep including performance and infrastructure harnesses that are too slow for per-PR execution.
Run mutation gates in parallel with functional eval suites, not sequentially. Both can start as soon as the fast gate passes.
Threshold Configuration

Define thresholds in a central config file referenced by all mutation CI jobs. Tiering by feature category avoids blanket gates that are either too strict (blocking unrelated infrastructure changes) or too lax (letting safety regressions through):
The Ratchet Rule

Absolute thresholds (e.g. always ≥ 85%) break when unrelated work temporarily makes it harder to achieve a score. The ratchet rule is a pragmatic alternative: the gate is the maximum of (a) the category minimum and (b) the last recorded score minus a small tolerance.

# eval/ratchet.py
import json, pathlib, sys

SCORE_LOG = pathlib.Path("eval/mutation_scores.jsonl")
FLOOR = 0.75          # absolute minimum — never go below this
TOLERANCE = 0.02      # allow at most a 2% drop per merge cycle

def load_last_score(category: str) -> float | None:
    if not SCORE_LOG.exists():
        return None
    entries = [json.loads(l) for l in SCORE_LOG.read_text().strip().splitlines()]
    category_entries = [e for e in entries if e.get("category") == category]
    if not category_entries:
        return None
    return category_entries[-1]["mutation_score"]

def compute_gate(category: str, minimum: float) -> float:
    last = load_last_score(category)
    if last is None:
        return minimum   # first run — use absolute minimum
    ratchet = last - TOLERANCE
    return max(minimum, ratchet, FLOOR)

def check_and_record(category: str, current_score: float, minimum: float) -> bool:
    gate = compute_gate(category, minimum)
    passed = current_score >= gate

    entry = {
        "category": category,
        "mutation_score": round(current_score, 4),
        "gate": round(gate, 4),
        "passed": passed,
    }
    with SCORE_LOG.open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")

    status = "PASS" if passed else "FAIL"
    print(f"[{status}] {category}: score={current_score:.1%} gate={gate:.1%}")
    return passed

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import argparse
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--category", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--score", type=float, required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--minimum", type=float, required=True)
    args = parser.parse_args()

    ok = check_and_record(args.category, args.score, args.minimum)
    sys.exit(0 if ok else 1)
CI Workflow Integration

# .github/workflows/mutation-gates.yml
name: Mutation Coverage Gates

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'eval/**'
      - 'prompts/**'
      - 'tests/eval/**'
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 2 * * *'    # nightly full sweep

jobs:
  fast-gate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: '3.12' }
      - run: pip install pytest coverage mutmut openai
      - name: Unit tests + coverage
        run: |
          coverage run -m pytest tests/eval/ -q
          coverage report --fail-under=95 --include="eval/*"
          coverage json -o eval/coverage.json

  mutation-gate:
    needs: fast-gate
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: '3.12' }
      - run: pip install pytest mutmut

      - name: Run mutmut on eval harness
        run: |
          mutmut run --paths-to-mutate eval/harness.py --tests-dir tests/eval/
          # Parse result
          SURVIVED=$(mutmut results 2>&1 | grep -c "^#" || echo 0)
          TOTAL=$(mutmut results --all 2>&1 | grep -c "^#" || echo 1)
          KILLED=$((TOTAL - SURVIVED))
          SCORE=$(python3 -c "print(round($KILLED / $TOTAL, 4))")
          echo "MUTATION_SCORE=$SCORE" >> $GITHUB_ENV

      - name: Ratchet gate check
        run: |
          python eval/ratchet.py             --category quality             --score $MUTATION_SCORE             --minimum 0.85

      - name: Annotate PR
        if: always() && github.event_name == 'pull_request'
        run: |
          STATUS="${{ job.status }}"
          GATE="85%"
          SCORE_PCT=$(python3 -c "print(f'{float("$MUTATION_SCORE")*100:.1f}%')")
          ICON="✅" && [ "$STATUS" = "failure" ] && ICON="❌"
          echo "::notice::Mutation score: $SCORE_PCT (gate: $GATE) $ICON"

  robustness-gate:
    needs: fast-gate
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: contains(github.event.pull_request.changed_files, 'prompts/')
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: '3.12' }
      - run: pip install openai

      - name: Run prompt mutation suite
        env:
          OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.OPENAI_API_KEY }}
        run: python eval/run_mutations.py

      - name: Ratchet gate check
        run: |
          SCORE=$(python3 -c "import json; r=json.load(open('eval/mutation_report.json')); print(r['robustness_score'])")
          python eval/ratchet.py             --category quality             --score $SCORE             --minimum 0.80

      - name: Upload mutation report
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: mutation-report-${{ github.run_number }}
          path: |
            eval/mutation_report.json
            eval/mutation_scores.jsonl
PR Annotations for Actionable Feedback
Raw mutation scores on CI are not actionable. The annotation must surface which mutants survived and what test each survivor implies:

# eval/annotate_pr.py
import json, os, subprocess

def get_survivors() -> list[dict]:
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["mutmut", "results", "--survived"],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    survivors = []
    for line in result.stdout.splitlines():
        line = line.strip()
        if line.startswith("#"):
            mutant_id = int(line.lstrip("#"))
            diff = subprocess.check_output(
                ["mutmut", "show", str(mutant_id)], text=True
            )
            survivors.append({"id": mutant_id, "diff": diff[:300]})
    return survivors

def annotate(report_path: str = "eval/mutation_report.json"):
    report = json.load(open(report_path))
    mutation_score = report["mutation_score"]
    gate = float(os.environ.get("MUTATION_GATE", "0.85"))
    passed = mutation_score >= gate
    icon = "checkmark" if passed else "X"

    print(f"Mutation score: {mutation_score:.1%}  Gate: {gate:.1%}  {icon}")

    if not passed:
        survivors = get_survivors()
        print(f"\nSurviving mutants ({len(survivors)}):")
        for s in survivors[:5]:   # show up to 5
            print(f"  Mutant #{s['id']}:")
            for line in s["diff"].splitlines()[:6]:
                print(f"    {line}")
            print(f"  --> Add a test that catches this mutation")
        if len(survivors) > 5:
            print(f"  ... and {len(survivors) - 5} more (see artifact)")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    annotate()
Exemptions and Suppression
Some mutations are logically equivalent to the original code (
equivalent mutants
) and will never be killed by any test. Counting them as survivors unfairly lowers the mutation score. Suppress them explicitly with a log entry, not by lowering the gate:
Score History Dashboard
A simple trend report helps teams see whether mutation coverage is improving over time:

# scripts/mutation_dashboard.py
import json, pathlib

LOG = pathlib.Path("eval/mutation_scores.jsonl")

def render_trend():
    if not LOG.exists():
        print("No history yet.")
        return
    entries = [json.loads(l) for l in LOG.read_text().strip().splitlines()]
    print(f"{'Date':<12} {'Category':<16} {'Score':>8}  {'Gate':>7}  {'Status':<6}")
    print("-" * 58)
    for e in entries[-20:]:   # last 20 runs
        status = "PASS" if e.get("passed", True) else "FAIL"
        print(
            f"{e.get('date','?'):<12} "
            f"{e.get('category','?'):<16} "
            f"{e['mutation_score']:.1%}  "
            f"{e.get('gate', 0.0):.1%}  "
            f"{status}"
        )

if __name__ == "__main__":
    render_trend()
Summary

Mutation coverage quality gates make the CI pipeline honest about the strength of the test suite. Line coverage tells you what code ran; mutation score tells you whether the assertions around that code actually work.
Use a multi-tier pipeline: fast gate first, mutation gate second, robustness gate third, full nightly sweep for slow categories.
Set tiered thresholds (90% safety / 85% quality / 75% performance) rather than a single blanket gate.
Apply the ratchet rule: gate = max(category_minimum, last_score - 2%) to prevent regression while avoiding brittleness.
Annotate PRs with surviving mutant diffs and specific fix instructions, not just a score.
Log equivalent-mutant suppressions with an expiry date; reject the CI run when suppressions have expired.
Track scores in a JSONL log and render a trend dashboard to surface long-term harness decay.
